Transcript:
Most sellers ruin a perfectly good
cadence because after four emails, they
keep selling for 10, 15, or 20 emails.
They keep pitching and pitching and
asking for time. What happens next is
predictable. You get unsubscribed, you
get moved to the junk folder, or the
sender name becomes [music] synonymous
with spam. So, in the nurture phase, we
stop trying to force a meeting and we
start [music] trying to earn their
trust. Your goal is simple. Add value
and educate. Stay in [music] their
orbit. Be the person they think of when
the timing shifts. And here's the most
important piece. Your emails will
[music] ask for nothing in return. Nada,
zilch, zero.
Every email from this point forward will
have no call to action. Remember that
your buyer's [music] silence is not a
no, it's a no for now without them
actually typing those words. Respect
their timing, but don't disappear.
Instead, simply add [music] value. You
want the sender's personal brand as a
seller and your company's brand to be
associated with being smart, helpful,
and relevant.
Nurturing is a fantastic way to do
[music] that and we want to keep the
structure intentionally simple. You
start with something [music] that you
came across that your buyer would
actually care about. Perhaps it's a
webinar, a short guide, a one-pager, a
blog post, a benchmark report, etc.
[music]
It has to be aligned with their role and
their world. This is also [music] a good
opportunity to partner with marketing.
Ask them what content is performing.
What's getting views? [music] What's
getting clicks? What's getting
registrations? What is being used in
late-stage deals?
But, unlike most nurture emails, we're
not going to send a link and run. No
one's going to open up a link in an
email unless they know why they should.
Next, give them a reason to look at the
resource you sent. One or so sentences
on why this is relevant to [music] them
is enough. And since you're such a
trusted source for them, add your point
of view. Lastly, give them a link and
sign off. If you want a gut check, read
your nurture email [music]
and ask yourself this. If a buyer
forwarded this email to a colleague,
would it feel like help or would it feel
like a disguised pitch? If it feels like
a pitch, we want to rewrite it. If
you're struggling to find content,
remember that almost anything that could
add value to this buyer can be used in a
nurture email.
>> [music]
>> That can be a post that your CEO did on
LinkedIn. It could be a third-party
article that was written on a topic
that's relevant to this buyer [music]
or even new releases of your products or
new product lines can be used as nurture
emails, as [music] well. Now, let's talk
about subject lines here again. Each of
these emails will have in your subject
[music] line. Remember that hook and
value format from before. They tell the
buyer what they will get if they open up
your email.
And here's a really [music] important
operational point. Because nurture is
value-driven, you'll often be able
[music] to reuse certain nurture emails
across more than one persona. Meaning,
emails five through [music] nine can
often work for several of your cadences
without having to constantly reinvent
the wheel, provided that that buyer
would be interested in the content.
